name: Curiosity Gap
template: The [thing] that [unexpected outcome] (and you're probably [doing it wrong])
example: The morning routine that ruins your productivity (and you're probably doing it every day)
notes: Use when reader already has familiarity with the topic but not the surprising angle. Triggers dopamine anticipation via information-gap theory — the brain experiences mild distress from incomplete information and compulsively seeks closure.
anatomy: Known subject + unexpected/negative outcome + implication that reader is affected

name: Pattern Interrupt
template: Stop. [Unexpected statement that breaks expectation].
example: Stop. Everything you know about building habits is backwards.
notes: Use at the very top of a piece to snap a scrolling reader out of passive consumption mode. Triggers an amygdala hijack — the brain's threat-detection system flags the unexpected input and forces attention.
anatomy: Jarring single-word command + bold contradiction of assumed context

name: Identity Hook
template: If you're [identity descriptor], you need to know this.
example: If you're a first-generation entrepreneur, you need to know this about funding.
notes: Use when you have a clearly defined audience segment. Activates mirror neurons and tribal identity circuitry — the reader self-selects in and feels the content was made specifically for them.
anatomy: Conditional identity qualifier + urgent implication that exclusive knowledge follows

name: Fear Hook
template: [Scary thing] is happening to [reader's group] right now.
example: A silent confidence killer is happening to high performers right now.
notes: Use when the problem is real, under-recognized, and your content offers the solution. Elevates cortisol and activates the brain's threat-detection system, making the reader feel compelled to read on to resolve the threat.
anatomy: Named threat + specific affected group + present-tense immediacy

name: Contrarian Hook
template: Everyone says [conventional wisdom]. They're wrong.
example: Everyone says you need to wake up at 5am to be successful. They're wrong.
notes: Use when you have genuine evidence or a reframe that challenges a widely held belief. Triggers novelty-driven dopamine release and opens a curiosity gap — the reader must know why the consensus is wrong.
anatomy: Stated common belief + blunt falsification

name: Social Proof Hook
template: [Large number] people can't all be wrong about [thing].
example: 47,000 founders can't all be wrong about this fundraising framework.
notes: Use when you have real, verifiable numbers to cite. Reduces ambiguity through social validation — the brain uses others' behavior as a cognitive shortcut for determining correct action.
anatomy: Specific large number + implied communal validation + named subject

name: Story Hook
template: The day [specific event] changed everything.
example: The day I lost my biggest client changed everything about how I run my business.
notes: Use to open long-form content, email sequences, or sales pages. Drops the reader in medias res, activating narrative transportation — story immersion suspends analytical judgment and lowers resistance to persuasion.
anatomy: Specific pivotal moment + consequence that implies transformation

name: Data Hook
template: In [year], [specific statistic] that nobody talks about.
example: In 2023, 78% of B2B deals stall at the proposal stage — a fact nobody in sales training talks about.
notes: Use when you have a genuinely surprising or under-reported statistic. Authority bias and specificity combine to build immediate credibility — the more precise the number, the more believable the claim.
anatomy: Temporal anchor + precise statistic + implied information gap

name: Open Loop
template: There are [N] reasons [outcome]. We'll get to the [Nth] one — the [surprising descriptor] one — at the end.
example: There are 5 reasons most diets fail. We'll get to the fifth one — the one no nutritionist will tell you — at the end.
notes: Use in list content, newsletters, and video scripts to drive completion rates. Exploits the Zeigarnik effect — the brain gives unfinished tasks preferential memory storage and creates a pull to close the loop.
anatomy: Numbered promise + withheld payoff + superlative qualifier on the withheld item

name: Embedded Command
template: As you read this, you begin to notice [desired state].
example: As you read this, you begin to notice how natural it feels to imagine running your own business.
notes: Use in sales copy, landing pages, and high-commitment CTAs. An NLP technique that embeds a direct command within a larger innocuous sentence structure, bypassing the critical faculty that would reject a blunt instruction.
anatomy: Presupposition frame + embedded imperative + desired outcome state

name: False Assumption Challenge
template: You think [thing], but that's actually not how [it] works.
example: You think posting more content builds an audience faster, but that's actually not how the algorithm works.
notes: Use when your content corrects a widely held misconception your audience is likely to hold. Triggers cognitive dissonance — the reader's existing mental model is challenged, and they must read on to update it.
anatomy: Attribution of false belief to reader + correction signal + subject domain

name: What If Hook
template: What if [conventional limitation] wasn't actually a constraint?
example: What if needing 8 hours of sleep wasn't actually a biological constraint?
notes: Use to open possibility-space content and reframe limiting beliefs. Opens a possibility space that didn't exist in the reader's mind a moment ago, triggering exploratory dopamine and inviting them to follow you into the unknown.
anatomy: Hypothetical inversion of accepted constraint + invitation to explore

name: Specificity Hook
template: [Hyper-specific detail or timeframe] instead of [vague equivalent].
example: In exactly 14 days — not 'in a few weeks' — here's the skin transformation protocol that actually worked.
notes: Use whenever you're tempted to use vague language like 'quickly' or 'soon.' Specificity signals that real experience or research is behind the claim — the brain uses precision as a proxy for credibility and truth.
anatomy: Precise number or qualifier + explicit contrast with vague alternative

name: Urgency & Scarcity Hook
template: Only [X] [spots/copies/hours] left. After that, [specific loss].
example: Only 12 spots left in the October cohort. After that, the next opening isn't until March.
notes: Use at decision points — checkout pages, email sequences, and event registration. Activates loss aversion circuits: the brain weights potential loss more heavily than equivalent gain, accelerating commitment decisions.
anatomy: Precise remaining quantity + named deadline or cutoff + specific consequence of missing out

name: Authority Hook
template: [Credentialed institution or person] found that [claim].
example: Harvard Business Review found that leaders who write daily are 3x more likely to reach executive roles.
notes: Use when you have a genuine citation from a respected source. Authority bias — a cognitive shortcut that assigns greater credibility to credentialed sources — reduces the reader's analytical resistance to your claim.
anatomy: Named authority source + empirical finding + implied significance for reader

name: Confession Hook
template: I have to be honest with you about [vulnerable admission].
example: I have to be honest with you: I built my first course while secretly terrified no one would buy it.
notes: Use to open content where trust is the primary barrier to conversion. Vulnerability triggers oxytocin release in the reader, creating a biochemical bond of intimacy and trust that makes subsequent claims far more persuasive.
anatomy: Honesty signal + specific personal admission that risks judgment

name: Before-After Hook
template: [Time period] ago I [was state A]. Today I [am state B]. Here's what changed.
example: 6 months ago I was billing $3,000/month. Today I charge $30,000 per project. Here's the one reframe that changed everything.
notes: Use on sales pages, testimonial sections, and transformation-oriented content. Activates identity resonance by showing a plausible transformation arc — the reader projects themselves into the 'before' and desires the 'after.'
anatomy: Specific time anchor + contrasting state A and state B + promise of bridge explanation

name: Secret Revelation Hook
template: What [authority/industry] doesn't want you to know about [thing].
example: What the fitness industry doesn't want you to know about why most gym memberships fail.
notes: Use when your content genuinely exposes a hidden mechanism or suppressed insight. Combines conspiracy framing with exclusivity dopamine — the promise of forbidden knowledge creates an urgent, almost compulsive need to read.
anatomy: Named withholding authority + forbidden knowledge signal + subject domain

name: Relatability Hook
template: You know that feeling when [specific relatable situation]?
example: You know that feeling when you've rewritten the same email four times and it still doesn't sound right?
notes: Use to instantly validate a shared frustration before introducing a solution. Activates mirror neurons — the brain simulates the described experience — and triggers the relief of recognition: 'I'm not the only one.'
anatomy: Shared-experience prompt + hyper-specific situation that only the target reader recognizes

name: Future Pacing
template: Imagine [specific time] from now, when you [desired outcome state]...
example: Imagine 90 days from now, when you open your laptop and your pipeline is already full before you send a single cold email...
notes: Use in sales copy, onboarding sequences, and lead nurture emails. Borrowed from NLP timeline therapy — it causes the reader to mentally rehearse the desired future, making it feel real and achievable, which lowers purchase resistance.
anatomy: Future time anchor + sensory-rich description of the desired outcome state

name: Loss Aversion Hook
template: Every day you wait, you're losing [specific thing].
example: Every day you wait to systematize your sales process, you're losing three to five deals you never even know about.
notes: Use as a bridge between problem-awareness and offer introduction. Draws on Kahneman's prospect theory — losses are neurologically weighted approximately twice as heavily as equivalent gains, making inaction feel increasingly costly.
anatomy: Time-based inaction frame + specific and tangible ongoing loss

name: Promise Hook
template: By the time you finish [this piece/video/course], you will [specific transformation].
example: By the time you finish this essay, you will have a complete framework for pricing your services without apology.
notes: Use at the start of any content that requires significant time investment. Establishes a clear upfront contract with the reader — the brain needs to know the value exchange before committing attention, and this hook provides it explicitly.
anatomy: Time-bounded completion frame + specific, concrete outcome promise

name: Question Hook
template: When was the last time you [specific meaningful action or state]?
example: When was the last time you closed your laptop feeling like you'd actually done your best work?
notes: Use to trigger immediate internal engagement before the reader has consciously decided to read. The brain cannot encounter a direct question without generating an answer — this automatic internal response creates instant engagement and emotional investment.
anatomy: Direct temporal question + specific action or state that implies a gap or longing

name: Bold Claim
template: [Extraordinary, provocative claim that demands proof].
example: Cold outreach is dead. And continuing to use it is actively damaging your brand.
notes: Use when you can fully back up an extraordinary claim within the piece. Shock creates cognitive dissonance — the gap between the stated claim and the reader's existing model demands resolution, generating a compulsive urge to read the justification.
anatomy: Short declarative sentence making an extraordinary claim + optional escalating implication

name: Empathy Hook
template: [Deeply accurate description of reader's pain or frustration] — I've been there.
example: You've built something real, but every time you try to explain it, people's eyes glaze over. I've been there.
notes: Use when your audience is in an emotionally charged state of frustration or overwhelm before you offer a solution. Validation before persuasion — when readers feel deeply understood, their defensive posture drops and they become far more open to what follows.
anatomy: Precise articulation of specific audience pain + solidarity signal from the writer

name: Paradox Hook
template: The [more/less] you [do X], the [opposite/counterintuitive result].
example: The more you try to convince people to buy, the less they trust you.
notes: Use when your content introduces a genuinely counterintuitive mechanism. Paradox creates cognitive tension — the logical contradiction occupies working memory and cannot be released until resolved, driving the reader to continue.
anatomy: Comparative intensifier + action + counterintuitive consequence

name: Transformation Hook
template: This is for people who are done being [state A] and ready to become [state B].
example: This is for people who are done being the best-kept secret in their industry and ready to become the obvious choice.
notes: Use at the top of sales pages, course landing pages, and community invitations. Issues an identity shift invitation — it positions the offer as a threshold the reader can choose to cross, activating aspiration and self-selection simultaneously.
anatomy: Audience qualifier + rejected identity state A + aspirational identity state B

name: Villain Hook
template: There's a reason [problem] keeps happening to [group]. And it's not [obvious cause].
example: There's a reason talented designers keep getting passed over for promotions. And it's not their portfolio.
notes: Use to open content that reframes the cause of a persistent problem and introduces a non-obvious solution. Common enemy framing externalizes blame and provides narrative relief, while the denial of the obvious cause opens a curiosity gap that demands resolution.
anatomy: Named persistent problem + affected group + subversion of obvious explanation
